title: Population of the Californias
subtitle: Every count in the archive: padrones, censos, and mission registros, 1777–1847
abstract: The population counts of Spanish and Mexican California, settlement by settlement, as the archive recorded them. A note on what these numbers are: they are the counts of a colonial bureaucracy. Padrones undercount, categories like gente de razon and neofito are the empire's constructs, and Native people appear only when and how the state counted them — inside missions, pueblos, and jurisdictions. The map shows what the documents say, in their own units, not how many people lived in California; the nations of the Native California map were mostly never counted at all. Every row cites its document. The headline: in 1790 the entire Spanish population of Alta California was 910 people.
legend_note: Figures are the documents' own; categories are the colonial state's.

features.name: Alta California (province-wide counts)
features.summary: The whole colony, counted. In 1790 the entire Spanish population of Alta California was 910 people, inside a mission population of 7,353. Duran's 1833 ledger then states the mission system's demographic arithmetic in three numbers: 82,882 baptized since founding, 58,417 dead, 10,482 living.
series: 8,323 souls total: 910 gente de razon + 7,353 Indios (per-establishment table)
mission ledger: 64,675 baptized / 41,767 died / 20,238 living since founding
24,990 habitantes: 21,196 neophytes in the 20 missions, 2,994 gente de razon — read into the Diputacion's founding session
'under 25,000, mostly Indians'; ~4,000 gente de razon, ~1,800 able to bear arms
school census: 11 schools, ~389 students in the whole territory
Duran's cumulative Cuadro Estadistico: 82,882 baptized / 58,417 died / 10,482 living
features.result: One province, three generations, counted into decline

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.notes: Placed at Monterey as the capital; the counts are province-wide.

features.name: Antigua (Baja) California (province-wide)
features.summary: The old province counted alongside the new: Arrillaga's 1791 census tables cover 18 poblaciones, presidio and missions, with the men, women, and children of each.
series: per-mission families/souls table, ~17 missions (Loreto mission 76 souls…)
province-wide censo y castas table, 18 poblaciones
the Dominican Prior's estado: 4,442 souls in the Baja missions
4,337 almas per the P. Presidente's mission states

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.notes: Placed at Loreto; see the individual Baja pins for 1790 figures.

features.name: Pueblo San José de Guadalupe
features.summary: The first pueblo, counted at 78 souls in 1790 — one europeo, 33 espanoles, 8 indios, 8 mulatos, 28 otras castas. Four decades later it had grown to 602.
series: founded with 14 vecinos, 66 souls (Neve's own report)
78 souls (1 europeo, 33 espanoles, 8 indios, 8 mulatos, 28 otras castas)
nominal padron of the pueblo (named households)
Padron General by Macario de Castro: 30 households, nominal, with demographic columns
345 souls (9 born, 3 died); Branciforte 67 and Los Ranchos 50 counted alongside
391 total: 59 men, 114 parvulos, 184 women and girls; + Rancho San Ysidro 66
602 souls: 431 gente de razon + 171 indigenas avecindados (two independent returns)
DSP Ben. (C-A 42) Doc 184; St. Pap. M&C (C-A 53) Doc 49

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Pueblo de Los Ángeles
features.summary: The pueblo's whole arc in five counts: 139 souls in the 1790 province table, 148 in 1792, 274 in 1817, then the Mexican-era growth to over a thousand — with the padrones counting Indians separately, in the pueblo and on its ranchos.
series: the founding padron de pobladores, each vecino by name with casta ('fundado el 4 de Set.e de 1781')
'el pueblo de los Angeles se compone de 8 habitantes' (vecino-pobladores, 3 years in)
pobladores census: 12 households with casta, age, occupation
nominal household roll: 31 families, 141 souls, with oficio/casta/age/birthplace
139 souls (province table); estado of population + property, named pobladores
148 souls (2 europeos, 57 espanoles, 17 mestizos, 15 indios, 57 mulatos)
274 souls in the pueblo, 537 in the jurisdiction ('no especifica los indios')

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.notes: The 1790 counts disagree: the province table says 139, the pueblo's own August roll 141 — both shown. The 1828 vs 1830 drop reflects changing padron scope as much as population.

features.name: Villa de Branciforte
features.summary: The third and smallest pueblo, founded 1797 across the river from Mission Santa Cruz. The 1823 padrones catch it at 128 souls, or 67 by the summer count — the two returns are shown as recorded.

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.notes: The June and December 1823 figures differ in scope; both preserved.

features.name: Monterey (town & jurisdiction)
features.summary: The capital counted as a town: the 1826 vecindario census breaks out gente de razon by sex and age beside the Indians living among them; by 1829 the town and its named ranchos held 867.
series: vecindario: gente de razon 75 men, 98 women, 82 boys, 56 girls (= 311) + Indians counted alongside
rancho census of the jurisdiction: 24 vecino-ranchos with titles

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Sacramento District (New Helvetia)
features.summary: The arc's endpoint: the December 1847 statistics of population of the Sacramento district, compiled at Sutter's fort in the first American winter — the count that closes the era this map covers.
series: the Sacramento-district census table ('Statistics of Population &c')

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Presidio of San Francisco
features.summary: The 1790 padron names every household of the company with casta, birthplace, age, wife, and children — 144 souls at the Golden Gate. By 1830 the whole SF jurisdiction, missions included, counted 5,469, of whom 5,240 were Indians.
series: 144 souls (1 europeo, 78 espanoles, 16 indios, 5 mulatos, 44 castas); nominal padron

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Presidio of Santa Bárbara
features.summary: The 1785 padron is a nominal roll of 67 heads of household — officers, sergeants, corporals, soldiers, vecinos, and the San Blas paquebot's servants — with casta, age, and family.
series: 67 heads of household, nominal roll with casta and family

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Presidio & town of San Diego
features.summary: San Diego's late-Mexican padrones count the men who could bear arms: 53 in 1845, 73 in 1846 — the municipality on the eve of the conquest.

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Presidio of Loreto (Baja)
features.summary: The old capital counted in 1790: 399 souls, a fifth of them under the fuero militar.
series: 399 souls (3 europeos, 73 espanoles, 152 indios, 10 mulatos, 158 otras castas)

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Real de Santa Ana (Baja mining district)
features.summary: The southern mining district, the most casta-diverse place in either California: 696 souls with 5 miners, 16 rancheros, and 93 others by trade.
series: 696 souls (3 europeos, 133 espanoles, 178 indios, 157 mulatos, 204 castas)

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.name: Mission San Carlos (Carmel)
features.summary: Neophyte population as the annual registros and the 1790 censo campaign recorded it.

sources.citation: All rows from the Archives of California survey catalogs; C-A 50 unless noted. Figures are the documents' own.

features.notes: The counts are of people living AT the mission when counted; flight, death, and new baptism churn beneath every figure. A 1795 nominal padron of the Monterey-district missions exists (C-A 50 Doc 54).
